title: GMAT Timing Strategy: How to Pace Yourself for a High Score
author: Tklesson Team
content:
The GMAT is as much a test of time management as it is of content knowledge. Every section is tightly timed, and the computer-adaptive format means you cannot skip a difficult question and return to it later. Once you submit an answer, it is final. The students who score highest are not necessarily those who know the most. They are those who manage their time well enough to apply what they know to every question in the section. Here is how to develop a pacing strategy that maximizes your score.

Understand the Timing Constraints

The GMAT Focus Edition has three sections, each forty-five minutes long. Quantitative Reasoning contains twenty-one questions. Verbal Reasoning contains twenty-three questions. Data Insights contains twenty questions. The average time per question is roughly two minutes across all three sections, but this average is misleading. Some questions can be answered in under a minute. Others require more time. The skill is knowing which is which and allocating your time accordingly.

The computer-adaptive format adds complexity. The difficulty of the questions you see depends on your performance on previous questions. Answering correctly leads to harder questions. Answering incorrectly leads to easier ones. Your final score reflects both the number of questions you answered correctly and the difficulty of those questions. This means the early questions in each section carry disproportionate weight. A careless error early in a section can route you into an easier question path from which recovery is difficult, even if you answer the later questions correctly.

Set Checkpoint Targets

For a twenty-one question Quantitative section, you should aim to complete question seven by the time fifteen minutes have elapsed, and question fourteen by the thirty-minute mark. For a twenty-three question Verbal section, similar checkpoints apply: roughly question eight at fifteen minutes, question sixteen at thirty minutes. For a twenty-question Data Insights section, aim for question seven at fifteen minutes and question fourteen at thirty minutes. These checkpoints are not rigid, but they give you a reference point to know whether you are ahead, on track, or falling behind.

If you are ahead of your checkpoint, maintain your pace. Do not rush. The extra time is a buffer for the harder questions later in the section. If you are behind, you need to speed up. This does not mean rushing through every remaining question. It means being more aggressive about guessing on questions that are taking too long and moving on. The goal is to reach the end of the section with enough time to attempt every question, even if some of your answers are guesses.

Know When to Guess and Move On

The most common timing mistake on the GMAT is spending too long on a single difficult question. A question that takes four minutes costs you more than the time itself. It costs you the opportunity to answer two or three easier questions later in the section. The expected value of guessing on the difficult question and investing the saved time in questions you are more likely to answer correctly is positive.

Set a hard time limit for any single question. Two and a half minutes. If you have not arrived at an answer by then, make your best guess and move on. This is psychologically difficult. Your instinct is to persist. You have invested time and mental energy. Walking away feels like admitting defeat. But the GMAT is not testing your persistence on individual questions. It is testing your ability to maximize your score across the entire section. A disciplined guess is a strategic decision, not a failure.

When you guess, do so intelligently. Eliminate any answer choices you can identify as clearly wrong. Even eliminating one or two options significantly improves your odds. Then select from the remaining choices and move forward without regret. Do not replay the question in your mind while working on the next one. The guess is made. The time is spent. Focus entirely on the question in front of you.

Practice Your Pacing Before Test Day

Pacing is a skill, and like any skill, it improves with practice. Do not wait until test day to discover whether you can manage your time effectively. Build pacing practice into your preparation from the beginning. When you work through practice questions, use a timer. When you take full-length practice tests, enforce the time limits strictly. Do not give yourself extra minutes. The discipline you develop in practice will serve you on test day.

After each practice session or mock test, review your timing. Did you meet your checkpoint targets? Where did you lose time? Were there specific question types that consistently took longer than they should? The timing review is as important as the content review. A student who consistently loses time on Data Sufficiency questions, for example, needs to practice evaluating sufficiency more efficiently or needs to be more willing to guess on difficult Data Sufficiency items.

Develop an internal clock. With enough timed practice, you will develop a sense of how long two minutes feels. You will know, without looking at the timer, when a question is taking too long. This internal clock is more reliable than glancing at the clock after every question, which itself consumes time and breaks your focus. Practice until the pacing feels natural.

The Bottom Line

Effective pacing on the GMAT requires checkpoint targets, a disciplined guessing strategy, and consistent timed practice. Set checkpoint targets for each section and monitor your progress against them. Set a hard time limit of two and a half minutes per question and guess when you exceed it. Practice your pacing in every study session and review your timing after every mock test. The students who score highest are not necessarily those who answer the most questions correctly. They are those who allocate their time wisely enough to attempt every question and avoid the costly errors that come from rushing through the final minutes of a section.
